Author: Samibare

### [Awkward Privilege](https://compete.metactf.com/611/problems#problem1676) 

Friday evening, ops rushed a development system into production, cutting a few corners to "just get it working".

One of the developers stashed something sensitive in their home directory, confident the lockdown was airtight.

We suspect there is a way to pivot to a higher-privileged account by exploiting misconfigured binaries.

Can you find any awkward misconfiguration to escalate privileges and read the flag?

---

**Before doing the challenge:**

As the description suggests, we will be looking for a misconfigured binary that we can utilize to retrieve the flag.  The process I will go through to address this challenge is to scan for misconfigured binaries, then utilize that to retrieve the flag. 

**Interacting with the shell:**

Accessing the website that is provided by the challenge, we will be welcomed by an interactive shell.

![][image1]

By moving around what we have, we can see that we have 3 users in the home directory. Going to the user dev, we see that there is a flag.txt. 

![][image2]

However, when trying to read the flag through 

cat flag.txt

We are told that we lack permission. 

![][image3]

**Scanning:** 

Since the cat command does not have high enough permission to read the flag, we must find another source. In order to search for a misconfigured binary, I am going to search the entire system that has a root setuid. I am simply going to utilize find to search for the misconfigured binary. The permissions of setuid files for root are 4000

find / \-perm \-4000 2\>/dev/null

![][image4]

The command exposes that gawk has root privilege.

**Exploiting the misconfigured binary:**

As we have gawk, we can simply use ‘{print}’ to return the content of the flag.txt

gawk ‘{print}’ flag.txt

Running this will return the flag.

![][image5]

MetaCTF{awkw4rd\_pr1v1l3g3\_3sc4l4t10n}

And that would wrap it up\!

**Technique:**

* Utilizing the find command to retrieve a file with a certain privilege: find / \-perm \-4000 2\>/dev/null  
  * Subtechnique: By having 2\>dev/null, we can make the find command not print out the errors and only print out the file that has matched the condition.

**What I learned:**

The exploitation and privilege escalation of doing what you're not allowed to do starts with scanning what is available to you. In this case, through scanning, I realized that gawk was available to my disposal. After digging into what gawk can do, I realized it can do many things, including reading a file. On a side note, by looking at GTFObins, I also learned that you can create a shell, reverse a shell, bind a shell, and read & write files. This reinforced the importance of having the correct knowledge base and knowing where to look to use what you have to its maximum potential.
